coredata.dc:title: Ti3C2 MXene-derived Li4Ti5O12 nanoplates with in-situ formed carbon quantum dots for metal-ion battery anodes
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Yong
Zhang, Wenhan
Lai, Chunyan
Yang, Tianrang
Chang, Xiwang
Zhang, Maohui
Sheng, Liangmei
Yang, Zhiyun
Ye, Daixin
Huang, Kevin
Xie, Jingying
coredata.dc:description: Two-dimensional (2D) material Ti3C2 MXenes have recently been used in electrode composites for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) for their excellent electrical conductivity and accordion-like nanosheet morphology. However, Ti3C2 has low specific capacity and fast degradation rate upon cycling after inevitably coupling with surface species during synthesis. In this work, Ti3C2 is used as Ti-source for Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) and C-source for carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in a one-step hydrothermal process. The resultant LTO product (M-LTO) inherits the nanosheet morphology of Ti3C2 with uniformly anchored CQDs. The highly electronic conductive CQDs optimize the transmission path of ions which reduces the diffusion barrier of ions, and they further increase the density of states of the material which effectively improving the conductivity of M-LTO. Remarkable electrochemical performances including high initial specific capacity, long lifetime and excellent low temperature capacity are demonstrated for this type of electrode in LIBs, sodium ion batteries (SIBs) and lithium-magnesium ion hybrid batteries (LMIHBs). This paper offers a new strategy to the rapidly expanding research on the application of transition metal MXenes in electrodes for metal-ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Ti3C2
Li4Ti5O12
Anode
Battery

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Energy Conversion and Storage and Environmental Technologies

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation processes of M-LTO. SEM images of (b) Ti3AlC2 and (c) Ti3C2. (d-g) SEM, TEM images of M-LTO.
(a) XRD patterns of Ti3C2, M-LTO and LTO. (b) XPS spectra of Ti for Ti3C2, LTO and M-LTO. (c) Raman spectra of LTO, M-LTO, Ti3C2 and Ti3AlC2. (d) Solid state NMR profile of M-LTO.

As a LIBs anode: (a) and (b) Cycle performance of LTO or M-LTO at 5C. (c) Rate performance of LTO and M-LTO. (d) Cycle performance of M-LTO at low temperature. (e) Nyquist plots of LTO and M-LTO electrodes before cycle. (f) Cyclic voltammograms (CVs) of M-LTO and LTO at different scan rates (The solid line represents M-LTO, the dashed line represents LTO).

The optimized Li-ion migration paths at (111) plane for Li11Ti13O32 and Li11Ti13O32-C41 from views of (a) (b) XOY and (c) (d) ZOX direction, respectively. The yellow spheres represent the transiting positions of Li-ion. (e) the DOS diagrams for (111) planes of Li11Ti13O32 and Li11Ti13O32-C41 at Li-Ti termination surface, and the PDOS diagram of C41 in the DOS of Li11Ti13O32-C41.
(a, b) Cycle performance and rate performance of LTO and M-LTO for sodium ion battery. (c, d) Cycle performance and rate performance of LTO and M-LTO for lithium-magnesium ion hybrid battery.

head.ce:title: Ti3C2 MXene-derived Li4Ti5O12 nanoplates with in-situ formed carbon quantum dots for metal-ion battery anodes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: g

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Two-dimensional (2D) material Ti3C2 MXenes have recently been used in electrode composites for lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) for their excellent electrical conductivity and accordion-like nanosheet morphology. However, Ti3C2 has low specific capacity and fast degradation rate upon cycling after inevitably coupling with surface species during synthesis. In this work, Ti3C2 is used as Ti-source for Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) and C-source for carbon quantum dots (CQDs) in a one-step hydrothermal process. The resultant LTO product (M-LTO) inherits the nanosheet morphology of Ti3C2 with uniformly anchored CQDs. The highly electronic conductive CQDs optimize the transmission path of ions which reduces the diffusion barrier of ions, and they further increase the density of states of the material which effectively improving the conductivity of M-LTO. Remarkable electrochemical performances including high initial specific capacity, long lifetime and excellent low temperature capacity are demonstrated for this type of electrode in LIBs, sodium ion batteries (SIBs) and lithium-magnesium ion hybrid batteries (LMIHBs). This paper offers a new strategy to the rapidly expanding research on the application of transition metal MXenes in electrodes for metal-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: The new two-dimensional (2D) Mxenes with combined metallic conductivity of transition metal carbides, nitrides or carbonitrides and hydrophilic nature of their terminated surfaces, distinguish them from their bulk counterparts [1–3]. Their accordion-like appearances together with the attractive mechanical, optical, magnetic and electronic properties have inspired many researchers to explore their applications in many areas, including sensors, transparent conductors, electromagnetic shielding, photoelectric catalysts for solar cells and electrodes in batteries [4–7]. Among these Mxenes, Ti3C2 has been predicted with a Li storage capability of 320 mAh g−1 by density functional theory (DFT) calculations [1]. However, Ti3C2 is almost exclusively prepared by removing the Al layers in Ti3AlC2 through HF etching, which leaves the terminated surfaces with -OH or -F functional groups [2]. The DFT calculated specific capacities are only 67 mAh g−1 for Ti3C2(OH)2 and 130 mAh g−1 for Ti3C2F2, consistent with the experimental result of 123.6 mAh g−1 at 1C rate for Ti3C2 anode [3].

On the other hand, improved battery performance has been reported for Ti3C2-containing anode composites by leveraging Mxenes’ suitable morphology, high electronic conductivity and mechanical strength [8–10]. Ai et al. prepared SnS-Ti3C2 composite by electrostatic adsorption, where the volume expansion of SnS during the cycle was effectively suppressed [11]. Fang et al. prepared a Ti3C2-coated carbon cloth by electroplating. The fast electronic conduction channels offered by Ti3C2 help induce the nucleation and lateral deposition of Na, thus avoiding dendritic formation [12]. Tang et al. prepared a Ti3C2/S conductive paper for Li–S batteries through a filtration-evaporation approach, which takes the advantages of the excellent conductivity and mechanical properties of Ti3C2 nanosheets [13].

We here report a different approach to utilizing Ti3C2 in batteries, in which Ti3C2 only serves as an intermediate for the synthesis of Li4Ti5O12 (LTO). The Ti3C2 with extracted Ti-ion serves as the raw material for a one-step hydrothermal process. The resultant LTO product (M-LTO) inherits the original morphology of Ti3C2 and contains the in-situ formed highly conductive phase, i.e., carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The advantages of M-LTO include (i) the nanosheets morphology increases the specific surface area, (ii) the uniformly anchored CQDs on the sheets enhance the electronic conduction, optimize the Li-ion diffusion pathway and decrease the Li-ion migration energy, (iii) CQDs are of lower weight and higher specific capacity than Ti3C2. We show that M-LTO exhibits impressive electrochemical performance when used asanode for metal-ion (Li+, Na+ and Li+-Mg2+) batteries.

Ti3C2 was obtained from the reaction between Ti3AlC2 powder and HF. Typically, 200 mg Ti3AlC2 was first immersed into 20 mL 48 wt% HF and kept stirring for 48 h at 25 °C . The solution was then filtered several times with deionized water. The obtained black powder was dried in an oven at 70 °C for 10 h.

M-LTO was obtained by a one-step hydrothermal method. First, 50 mL absolute ethanol and 0.5 mL deionized water were added to a beaker, followed by adding 2.382 g tetrabutyl titanate (TBT) drop-wise and kept stirring for1 h. After that, 2 mL deionized water was added under stirring. The obtained solution was filtered and then placed in an oven at 80 °C for 3 h. The obtained product was named as powder A. 0.018 g Ti3C2 was added in another beaker with 70 mL deionized water and kept stirring for 10 min. Then 0.018 g sodium dodecyl benzene sulfonate (SDBS) and 0.587 g lithium hydroxide (LiOH·H2O) were added to the above solution separately and stirred for 30 min. After that, powder A was added to above solution and kept stirring for 3 h. Finally, the solution was poured into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 150 °C for 14 h. After the hydrothermal process, the product was repeatedly rinsed with absolute ethanol and deionized water to remove residual SDBS and then placed in an oven of 80 °C for 2 h. At last, the sample was placed in a tube furnace and heated to 550 ℃ with a heating rate of 2 °C min−1 under Ar and annealed for 6 h. The final product was named as M-LTO. On the other hand, Li4Ti5O12 was also prepared by the same way without adding Ti3C2, and the product was named as LTO.

The morphology was captured by scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi S-4800) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL 2100F). The specific surface area of the material was measured by BET analysis (Micromeritics ASAP 2020 M). X-ray diffraction (XRD Rigaku D/max-2600pc) was used to examine the crystal structure of the materials (2θ ranges from 5° to 80°). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo K-Alpha XPS spectrometer) was conducted to confirm the chemical state of the samples. Raman spectroscopy was carried out using Raman spectroscope (HORIBA Scientific Instruments Division) to examine the state of carbon. 13C Magic Angle Spinning Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (MAS NMR) measurement was conducted on a Bruker Avance III 400 spectrometer equipped with 89 mm wide-bore 9.4 T superconducting magnets. The single pulse spectrum of M-LTO powder was collected at the Larmor frequency of 100.6 MHz with the recycle delay of 2.0 s and the spinning speed of 12 kHz. 13C chemical shift was referenced to adamantine at 38.5/29.5 ppm. The carbon content in the M-LTO was checked by Elemental analyzer (Vario EL cube).

The first-principles calculations were carried out to obtain DOS and transition states of two different structures, LTO with/without CQDs, respectively. The results were obtained by means of first-principle calculation procedure as implemented in the computer code CASTEP of Material Studio. The exchange-correlation PBE functional was of the GGA-type. In the BFGS, the kinetic energy cut-off for the plane wave basis was set to 340 eV and the convergence precision energy was less than 1.0 × 10−5 eV/atom. Ultimate atom displacement was less than 0.001 Å, max force between each atom were less than 0.03 eV/Å and stress of virtual process were less than 0.05 GPa, respectively. κ-point grids were 2x2x2, the slab model with vacuum of 15 Å were used to simulate the LTO (111 plane). Complete LST/QST method was used to calculate the migration paths and barriers for Li-ion migration.

The anode was prepared by mixing active material, acetylene black and PVDF (the ratio is 80 wt%:10 wt%:10 wt%). The mixture was dispersed in N-methyl pyrrolidone (NMP) to form a homogeneous slurry. Subsequently, the slurry was uniformly coated on the cleaned copper foil and then placed in an oven at 80 °C overnight. After that, the electrodes were pressed into slice (diameter = 14 mm, the mass of active material on each electrode is approximately 1.0–1.2 mg). The Celgard 2400 polyethylene was used as separator for lithium ion battery and the glass fiber (Whatman) was used as separator for sodium ion battery and lithium-magnesium ion hybrid battery. Li, Na and Mg metal were used as the counter electrode respectively to assemble the CR2016-type coin cells in glove box. The electrolyte was prepared by dissolving 1 M LiPF6 in EC, EMC and DMC (volume ratio of 1:1:1), 0.9 M NaPF6 in DME and 1 M APC in THF with 0.4 M LiCl for lithium ion battery, sodium ion battery and lithium-magnesium ion hybrid battery, respectively. Electrochemical performance tests for lithium ion battery were performed at different rates (1C = 175 mA g−1) between 1.0 V and 2.5 V with Land CT2001A tester (sodium ion battery and lithium-magnesium ion hybrid battery were tested between 0.5 and 2.5 V and 0.01–1.85 V, respectively). The CV (0.1 mV s−1) and EIS (10−2 Hz to 105 Hz) tests were conducted by CHI1000E and CHI660E electrochemical workstation, respectively. The cryogenic cycle was tested in the CT4005 low temperature test box.

The preparation processes are schematically showed in Fig. 1
a. The Al layers in Ti3AlC2 are first removed by HF etching. The obtained Ti3C2 is then mixed with LiOH·H2O and tetrabutyl titanate (TBT) for hydrothermal reactions, followed by annealing at 550 °C. The SEM images in Fig. 1 b and 1c show that the original compacted nanosheets in Ti3AlC2 converts to accordion-like nanolayers in Ti3C2, indicating that Al layers have been successfully removed by HF. The obtained M-LTO powder after hydrothermal process consists of many nanosheets with a thickness of 30 nm (Fig. 1 d and Fig. 1 e). For comparison, the LTO powder synthesized under the same hydrothermal conditions but without the assistance of Ti3C2 shows a morphology of agglomerated nanoparticles ( Figure S1 ). M-LTO shows a much higher specific surface area of 79.1 m2 g−1 than LTO (56.6 m2 g−1) from BET test ( Figure S2 ). Many quantum dots are found evenly distributed on the M-LTO nanosheets, see the TEM image in Fig. 1 f. The HRTEM in Fig. 1 g shows that the lattice fringe in the matrix has a width of 0.48 nm, which can be assigned to the (111) plane of Li4Ti5O12. The d-spacing of 0.212 nm in the quantum dots cannot be indexed into planes belonging to Li4Ti5O12 or Ti3C2, but the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [14,15].

The XRD pattern of M-LTO in Fig. 2
a also shows no diffraction peaks from Ti3C2 but only LTO, both of which can be indexed into the spinel phase (Fd3m) [16,17]. EDS under SEM mode was also performed to investigate the elements distribution in the M-LTO, as shown in Figure S3 . In M-LTO, Ti, O and C elements distribute uniformly, indicating the carbon materials may exist in the M-LTO. The Ti-2p XPS spectra of both LTO and M-LTO in Fig. 2 b show typical photoemission peaks of spinel phase at 458.3 eV and 464.1 eV [18]. The fitting results of Ti-2p spectra in Ti3C2 indicate that the surface of Ti3C2 is rich in dangling bonds. Four split peaks located at 454.4, 455.4, 456.6 and 459 eV are revealed by the spectra fitting, which can be assigned to Ti-C bond, Ti-X bond (a combination of substoichiometric TiCx (x < 1) and titanium oxycarbides (TiCxOy)), reduced charge state bond in TixOy and regular Ti-O, respectively [16,19,20]. The Raman spectra in Fig. 2 c shows two obvious peaks at 1590 and 1340 cm−1 for both M-LTO and Ti3C2, respectively, corresponding to the typical G and D bands of carbon species [21]. They are not present in the spectra of LTO or Ti3AlC2, which rules out the possibility of carbon residues during the synthesis processes of M-LTO or the commercial Ti3AlC2. In addition, although SDBS is added as a surfactant in the preparation process, repeated rinse was carried out after the hydrothermal processes, which can also rule out the possibility of SDBS as a carbon source. The intensity ratio of IG/ID for M-LTO is much higher than that of Ti3C2, suggesting that the carbon evolves into a higher graphitization degree during the hydrothermal reaction and subsequent annealing. 13C NMR spectra in Fig. 2 d further confirms the existence of carbon in the M-LTO. The carbon content in the M-LTO is 0.16 wt% (Table S1). In the fluorescent emission spectra of M-LTO composite (Figure S4), the sample exhibit an emission peak at about 459 nm, which is a typical feature of carbon quantum dots, further confirming the existence of carbon quantum dots. The above results indicate that Ti3C2 has partially decomposed into carbon species, such as TiCx, TiCxOy and TixOy, after the HF-etching; Ti-ion further participates into the hydrothermal reaction to form the spinel LTO; large amount of carbon with unsaturated bonds is then left to react with each other during the subsequent synthesis processes to form the CQDs inside the LTO matrix.

Fig. 3
a shows the cycle performance of LTO and M-LTO at 5C in LIBs coin half-cells. The specific discharge capacity (SDC) of M-LTO in the first cycle at 5C reaches 175.4 mAh g−1, which is almost identical to the theoretical value of 175 mAh g−1 for spinel LTO [22]. The capacity retention rate (CRR) is 95.5% after 500 cycles. For LTO, the initial SDC is only 153.9 mAh g−1 with a CRR of 91% under the same conditions. An extended life test of 5000 cycles at 5C shows that the SDC for M-LTO is still 160.7 mAh g−1, corresponding to a CRR of 91.3% (Fig. 3 b). M-LTO also shows obvious advantages over LTO at high rates, see Fig. 3 c. M-LTO can still deliver SDCs of 168.8 and 168.4 mAh g−1 at 30 and 40C, respectively. The low temperature cycling performance of M-LTO electrode at 0.5C is showed in Fig. 3 d. The SDC are barely impacted by the temperature, i.e. 169.6, 164.3 and 162.8 mAh g−1 at -10, -20 and −30 °C, respectively. Table S2 compares our results with the recent works to further demonstrate the superior performance of M-LTO. It can be found that all the LTO@carbon anodes in the Table S2 manifested good rate capability, indicating that carbon in the composite can promote the electron transport. In addition, the M-LTO in this work shown 168.4 mAh g−1 at 40C, which is higher than those reported literatures [23–28]. Furthermore, the M-LTO demonstrated better cycling stability. The excellent rate capability and cycling performance may be attributed to the improved ion transport kinetics owing to the carbon quantum dots embedded on the surface of LTO nanosheets.

The EIS spectra and the equivalent circuit fitting are showed in Fig. 3 e for both LTO and M-LTO before cycling. Inside the circuit, R1 represents the electrolyte impedance; R2 represents the charge transfer impedance; W1 represents the Warburg impedance [17]. The charge transfer process is obviously easier at the M-LTO/electrolyte interface than that in the LTO cell. The kinetic characteristic of the as-prepared cells is also analysed by cyclic voltammetry (CV) (Fig. 3 f). The Li+ diffusion coefficient DLi+ is correlated with the peak current by [14],
(1)
i p
=
2.69 ×
10 5
n
3 / 2
A
D
Li +
1 / 2
ν
1 / 2
C
Li +
where n is the stoichiometric number of electrons involved in the electrode reaction; A represents the surface area of the electrode; ν represents the scanning rate, and CLi+ is molar concentration of Li+ in solid. DLi+ can be calculated from the slope of ν1/2-ip curve. The fitting curves are showed in Figure S5 . The calculated DLi+ of the LTO and M-LTO are 3.09 × 10−13 and 1.2 × 10−12 cm2 s−1, respectively. Hence, the excellent electrochemical performance of M-LTO can be attributed to nanosheets structure decorated with CQDs, which effectively improves the Li+ and electron transportations.

The density functional theory (DFT) calculation is used to further study the role of CQDs. To simulate the composition in M-LTO, a C41 is put on top of the (111) plane with Li-Ti termination of Li11Ti13O32 superlattice. (We calculated two different cases, where the (111) plane of Li11Ti13O32 is combined with C24 and C41 atoms respectively. After comparison, the binding energy of C41 is more negative, that is, the binding of C41 is better. Therefore, C41 was selected as the model for this calculation.) This termination is selected because of its lowest surface energy when compared to other surfaces or terminations. The optimized Li-ion migration paths are shown in Fig. 4 for both Li11Ti13O32 and Li11Ti13O32-C41. It can be clearly observed that the migration path of Li-ion in Li11Ti13O32 is mainly undulating in a semi-circular arc along the z direction, which is approximately a straight line when viewed along the z direction. The undulation in the z-direction becomes smaller after the carbon is added. It mainly shows a semicircular arc along the XOY plane. The calculated energy barriers from transition state to reactant and product for Li11Ti13O32 are 1.366 and 1.364 eV, respectively, compared to 0.652 and 0.811 eV for Li11Ti13O32-C41. The smaller barrier means that carbon can effectively improve the Li-ion diffusion kinetics. In addition, the density of states (DOS) calculation indicates the electronic conductivity can be largely improved by carbon loading. As shown in Fig. 4 e, the electron density increases near the Fermi level and the energy gap in Li11Ti13O32 is filled after adding C41. The PDOS (partial density of states) diagram of C41 shows that it fills this gap, indicating that CQDs can effectively increase the electronic conductivity of LTO.

Finally, the LTOs are explored in other metal-ion batteries to demonstrate the universality of the materials designed here. M- LTO shows even more advantages over LTO in sodium ion batteries (SIBs) and lithium-magnesium ion hybrid batteries (LMIHBs). The typical charge-discharge curves are shown in Figure S6
a and b. As shown in Fig. 5
a, the M-LTO delivers a significantly improved SDC of 177 mAh g−1 with a CRR of 85.3% after 450 cycles at 1C in SIBs, compared to SDC of 126 mAh g−1 and CRR of 76.5% for LTO. Fig. 5 b shows that the M-LTO also exhibits a better rate capacity and reversibility than LTO. M-LTO still maintains a SDC of 92.3 mAh g−1 at 20C, whereas LTO only delivers 54.4 mAh g−1. When applied in LMIHBs, the M-LTO shows an initial SDC of 185.2 mAh g−1 at 100 mA g−1 with a CRR of 93.2% after 250 cycles (Fig. 5 c). An activation period during the first several cycles is also observed, where the SDC further increases to 196.8 mAh g−1. Superior rate performance is also observed for M-LTO. The SDCs are 180.6, 184.2, 181, 179 and 178 mAh g−1 at current densities of 50, 100, 200, 300 and 400 mA g−1, respectively (Fig. 5 d). In addition, the low-temperature performance of M-LTO for LMIHBs at 100 mA g−1 was also tested and the results are shown in Figure S6
c and d. The discharge capacity of M-LTO can still reach 131.1 mAh g−1 at -20 ℃. As a result, the M-LTO exhibits excellent cycling stability, rate and low-temperature capabilities (Table S3).

CQDs modified Li4Ti5O12 (LTO) has been synthesized by the hydrothermal method with 2D Mxene Ti3C2 as the precursor. The modified LTO (M-LTO) inherits the nanosheets morphology of Ti3C2 which increases the specific surface area over the pristine LTO. The CQDs improve the kinetics of the charge transfer process and the rate of Li-ion diffusion, as evidenced by both experimental and density functional theory (DFT) calculation. In addition, CQDs also have higher specific capacity and better stability than Ti3C2, which makes it more promising than recently reported electrode-Ti3C2 composite. M-LTO as a LIBs anode exhibits remarkable electrochemical performances with an initial capacity identical to the theoretical value of 175 mAh g−1 and a capacity retention rate of 91.3% after 5000 cycles at 5C. The discharge specific capacity can still reach 162.8 mAh g−1 even at -30 °C. Moreover, M-LTO can also work efficiently as anodes for SIBs with a discharge capacity of 177 mAh g−1 at 1C and a retention rate of 85.3% after 450 cycles and for LMIHBs with a discharge of 185.2 mAh g−1 at a current density of 100 mA g−1 and a capacity retention rate of 93.2% after 250 cycles. The strategy of synthesizing LTO nanoplates from Ti3C2 MXene with in-situ formed CQDs is also applicable to other transition metal MXenes and electrodes.
The manuscript was written through contributions of all authors. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

Yong Li: Investigation, Methodology. Wenhan Zhang: Investigation, Methodology. Chunyan Lai: Investigation, Methodology. Tianrang Yang: Investigation, Methodology. Xiwang Chang: Project administration. Maohui Zhang: Validation. Liangmei Sheng: Project administration. Zhiyun Yang: Validation. Daixin Ye: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Kevin Huang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jingying Xie: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFB0905300, 2018YFB0905301), Natural Science Foundation of Shanghai (21ZR1443200), the Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality (20511107800) and Hong Kong, Macao and Taiwan Science and Technology Coopen ratio Project of Shanghai Science and Technology Commission (19160760700).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2022.09.040.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Are MXenes promising anode materials for Li ion batteries? Computational studies on electronic properties and Li storage capability of Ti3C2 and Ti3C2X2 (X = F, OH) monolayer
J. Am. Chem. Soc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Q. Tang, Z. Zhou, P. Shen, Are MXenes promising anode materials for Li ion batteries? Computational studies on electronic properties and Li storage capability of Ti3C2 and Ti3C2X2 (X = F, OH) monolayer, J Am Chem Soc 134 (2012) 16909-16916.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-dimensional nanocrystals produced by exfoliation of Ti3AlC2
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Naguib, M. Kurtoglu, V. Presser, J. Lu, J. Niu, M. Heon, L. Hultman, Y. Gogotsi, M.W. Barsoum, Two-dimensional nanocrystals produced by exfoliation of Ti3AlC2, Adv Mater 23 (2011) 4248-4253.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-dimensional Ti3C2 as anode material for Li-ion batteries
Electrochem. Commun.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Sun, M. Wang, Z. Li, G. Fan, L.-Z. Fan, A. Zhou, Two-dimensional Ti3C2 as anode material for Li-ion batteries, Electrochemistry Communications 47 (2014) 80-83.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultrastable MXene@Pt/SWCNTs' Nanocatalysts for Hydrogen Evolution Reaction
